title: Storming The Castle

jokes.text: Michael doing 'a little fun' before work, which turns out to be cost-projection analysis for a mini-mall
jokes.setup: Narrator says Michael decided to have 'a little fun' before going to work, building expectation of leisure
jokes.punchline: The 'fun' is cost-projection analysis for a mini-mall — pure dry comedy about Michael's workaholic, joyless personality
jokes.explanation: Classic Arrested Development ironic narrator move. The joke is perfectly calibrated to Michael's character — his idea of fun is a work task. Strong character integration, economical, and the narrator delivery makes it land cleanly.

jokes.text: Wow. That's pretty cool you know how to do all that stuff. / Yeah. Well, maybe your old man is just a little bit cooler than you thought he was.
jokes.setup: George Michael's low bar compliment — being impressed by basic spreadsheet work
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately puffs up with pride at being praised for something utterly mundane, calling himself 'a little bit cooler'
jokes.explanation: Efficient character moment. Michael's need to impress his teenage son with workplace competence is very true to the show's portrait of his sad coolness. The 'little bit cooler' phrasing is perfectly deflating.

jokes.text: Stupid. Stupid chair. Very stupid chair.
jokes.setup: Michael brags about being cool, then immediately the cheap model-home chair malfunctions on him
jokes.punchline: His impotent muttering at an inanimate object undercuts his self-congratulation instantly
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed visual/physical beat. The escalating self-blame directed at an inanimate object ('Very stupid chair') is economically absurd and lands as a physical punctuation to his hubris. Sets up the entire leather chair arc of the episode.

jokes.text: Yeah, but isn't that why Grandpa's in jail, because he took things from work?
jokes.setup: Michael justifies taking a chair from work because he's president
jokes.punchline: George Michael cuts right to the obvious irony — George Sr. is in jail for the same reason
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's sharpest bits. George Michael, the literal child, instantly sees the logic Michael is too self-serving to acknowledge. The parallel to George Sr.'s crimes lands economically and is deeply character-specific. The show's central theme (the family's moral blindness) compressed into one line from the youngest character.

jokes.text: Not stealing. Not. I'm the one that taught you stealing is bad, all right?
jokes.setup: George Michael correctly identifies that taking the chair is theft
jokes.punchline: Michael defensively insists he taught George Michael stealing is wrong — while actively planning to steal
jokes.explanation: Michael's emphatic self-contradiction — the stammer of 'Not stealing. Not.' — is a beautifully petulant non-argument. He's caught and he knows it, but doubles down. Very true to the character's self-delusion.

jokes.text: Leather chair? So, you're against stealing, but skinning cows is cool with you.
jokes.setup: Michael wants to get a nice leather chair from work
jokes.punchline: George Michael reframes 'leather' as cow-skinning, catching Michael in a different moral inconsistency
jokes.explanation: Excellent lateral thinking joke. The reframe from 'leather chair' to 'skinning cows' is fast, unexpected, and logically airtight. George Michael catches Michael in a second moral blind spot just as the first one was resolved. Very sharp construction.

jokes.text: I'm fine, by the way. Frankly, your concern is getting embarrassing.
jokes.setup: Michael has just fallen out of the cheap model-home chair (inferred from earlier complaint and the chair going 'Oh, this one's gonna go')
jokes.punchline: He addresses his fall as though George Michael's concern is the problem — deadpan pride over physical mishap
jokes.explanation: The line is classic Arrested Development — addressing a physical moment with completely inverted priority. 'Frankly, your concern is getting embarrassing' is very quotable and economical. Michael's dignity policing is the joke.

jokes.text: I'm not against the insides. People need meat to survive. You are aware they don't remove it from the cow surgically, right?
jokes.setup: Michael says he's not against leather, just the outside of cows; George Michael asks if he's a vegetarian
jokes.punchline: Michael's bizarre distinction between 'insides' and 'outsides' of a cow, met by George Michael's perfectly patient reality check
jokes.explanation: The internal logic of 'I'm not against the insides' is absurd and delightful. Michael genuinely believes he's found a distinction. George Michael's deadpan correction ('You are aware they don't remove it from the cow surgically, right?') is surgically placed.

jokes.text: Michael wished Marta was his girlfriend. A secret he had only shared with Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Michael warmly greets Marta (Gob's girlfriend), appearing friendly and brotherly
jokes.punchline: Narrator reveals Michael secretly wants Marta for himself — and has already told his sister, making it only half a secret
jokes.explanation: The narrator's aside is perfectly constructed. 'A secret he had only shared with Lindsay' undercuts 'secret' with a perfect non-secret — the joke lives in the gap between the word and the reality. Classic AD narrator usage: reveal, undercut, move on instantly.

jokes.text: Neither would I. What career? The magic? Oh, the tricks. The little tricks.
jokes.setup: Marta says she'd never belittle Gob's career. Michael agrees he wouldn't either
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately belittles Gob's career in the same breath — 'the little tricks' is a particularly withering diminishment
jokes.explanation: The speed of the self-contradiction is the joke. Michael says 'Neither would I' and before the sentence ends is already doing it. 'The little tricks' is a great throwaway insult that doesn't even feel like one to Michael.

jokes.text: Real needle. Real apple. Real neck. Él es zombi!
jokes.setup: Marta describes Gob's new magic trick that upset her and the kids
jokes.punchline: The escalating 'real' items — needle, apple, neck — reveal it was a grotesque illusion on children, ending in a child screaming 'He's a zombie!'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The three-beat escalation (needle → apple → neck) is textbook comic rhythm, each word landing harder than the last. The Spanish 'Él es zombi!' from a traumatized child is the perfect absurdist punctuation. Economical to the point of genius — six words plus a reaction recreate an entire scene. The darkness (Gob doing gore magic for children) is very on-brand.

jokes.text: Oh, sure. First you dump all over it, now you wanna know how it's done.
jokes.setup: Marta says the kids were scared by the trick; Michael asks how it's done
jokes.punchline: Michael defends Gob's magic despite having just dismissed it as 'little tricks,' suddenly fascinated by the mechanics
jokes.explanation: Solid if minor beat — Michael's hypocrisy is called out implicitly but the joke relies on the viewer tracking his quick reversal. Works but is the weakest of the opening run.

jokes.text: He is staying here. I just haven't seen him here... the foyer or the kitchen.
jokes.setup: Marta worries Gob might be with another woman since he said he'd be staying at the model home
jokes.punchline: Michael covers for Gob with a non-denial denial — technically true, implying the house has been checked, when actually Gob isn't there
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'the foyer or the kitchen' signals Michael improvising his lie in real time. Adding specific rooms he hasn't checked is a nice comic detail — it sounds thorough while meaning nothing.

jokes.text: Tell her Gob is not staying here? Tell her Gob is screwing around on her? God knows where he is? Actually, that-that sounded okay.
jokes.setup: George Michael challenges Michael on why he protected Gob from Marta
jokes.punchline: Michael runs through the obvious truths he could tell Marta, dismissing them — then catches himself as the third option actually sounds reasonable, undercutting his own nobility
jokes.explanation: The self-interrupting 'Actually, that-that sounded okay' is a nice piece of character comedy. Michael realizes mid-speech that his noble instincts are actually sabotaging him, and the moment of self-awareness is quickly buried.

jokes.text: The noble one. The one who never wins. The loser. The fool.
jokes.setup: George Michael calls Michael 'the noble one' and 'the one who never wins' as compliments
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from 'noble' to 'loser' to 'fool' — George Michael doesn't notice the journey, Michael's 'Hmm?' signals he does
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'noble' to 'the fool' while George Michael maintains the same sincere tone is excellent. The 'Hmm?' from Michael as it lands on 'fool' is the visual/reaction beat that sells it. George Michael's complete unawareness of the insult is very true to his character.

jokes.text: Lindsay had always been celebrated for her wine and cheese charity fund-raisers.
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains that her mother pretends to care about causes she doesn't actually support
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Lindsay's own 'charitable' work is wine-and-cheese fund-raisers — all party, no cause
jokes.explanation: Superb narrator irony. Lindsay calling out her mother's performative charity is immediately undermined — her own 'charity' is literally a party. The narrator's deadpan 'celebrated' is doing heavy lifting. Strong character integration because this is the most Lindsay thing possible.

jokes.text: George Michael had been trying to prove to Maeby that he was macho, after an embarrassing brush with her. 'Your legs look exactly like mine, and I just shaved mine.'
jokes.setup: Narrator explains George Michael wants to prove he's macho to Maeby
jokes.punchline: The 'embarrassing brush' is revealed to be Maeby pointing out their legs look the same because she just shaved hers — implying George Michael's legs are already smooth/unmanly
jokes.explanation: The setup/reveal structure works perfectly — 'embarrassing brush' teases, then the actual quote lands as a precise, devastating image. It's specific enough to be genuinely funny and perfectly characterizes both George Michael (hapless, un-macho) and Maeby (casually withering).

jokes.text: I'm gonna need a leather jacket when I'm on my hog and need to go into a controlled slide.
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to seem macho so he announces he's getting a motorcycle
jokes.punchline: His reasoning — needing a leather jacket for 'a controlled slide' on 'my hog' — sounds like he's read exactly one article about motorcycles and is repeating it verbatim
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'controlled slide' is the comic gem here — it's the kind of hyper-specific technical term a teenager who doesn't own a motorcycle would deploy. 'My hog' from George Michael is inherently funny. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: But you have every day off. You don't have a job.
jokes.setup: Tobias bursts in announcing he has 'the entire day off' as great news
jokes.punchline: Lindsay flatly points out he has every day off because he's unemployed
jokes.explanation: A beautifully simple line. The complete absence of enthusiasm or cruelty — just factual correction — is perfectly Lindsay. 'You don't have a job' lands hard because it's delivered with zero drama. Economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no. I can't do that. / The next day, Michael was stopped on his way to work.
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Maeby say they're going shopping; Tobias says 'Oh, no, no. I can't do that.' — implying he has somewhere important to be
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately cuts to the next day, implying Tobias had no plans — his protest was meaningless
jokes.explanation: A minor structural gag — the narrator's cut dismisses Tobias's dramatic refusal. The joke is in the juxtaposition but it's fairly subtle and easy to miss.

jokes.text: Get in. / What? / If you care about your brother, you'll get in this car. / Which brother?
jokes.setup: A menacing figure in a limo tells Michael to get in the car for his brother's sake
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately asks 'Which brother?' — a family so dysfunctional that a threatening summons about an unspecified brother doesn't even narrow it down
jokes.explanation: Two words ('Which brother?') do enormous work. It's both a character joke (Michael has SO many troubled brothers) and a world-building joke about the Bluth family dysfunction. The set-up plays like a standard thriller beat, the punchline is pure comedy. Excellent economy and originality.

jokes.text: Tell him Rollo wants him to say good-bye to his legs.
jokes.setup: Rollo delivers a theatrical mob-style threat about legs — played straight, sounds violent
jokes.punchline: Later revealed to be about the 'legs' prop from Gob's magic trick — complete genre subversion
jokes.explanation: The payoff of this joke comes much later (at 07:47) when we learn 'the legs' are the bottom half of Gob's magic trick box. The construction across the episode is excellent — a genuine thriller line that turns out to be absurdly mundane. The rewatch bonus is high because on second viewing, the line is immediately funny for the right reason.

jokes.text: I'm so glad you're here. I want you to help me break up your brother and his girlfriend. / Well, I'm all for that. / He's locked on the balcony. / Oh. You meant Buster.
jokes.setup: Lucille says she needs Michael's help to break up a brother from his girlfriend — Michael assumes it's Gob (he's already thinking about that)
jokes.punchline: The reveal that it's Buster locked on the balcony; Michael assumed wrong brother again
jokes.explanation: Wait — correcting is_standup to false. This is a sitcom scene. The 'wrong brother' callback to joke 22 ('Which brother?') gives this extra layering. Lucille's solution of locking Buster on the balcony is also funny as background information.

jokes.text: Tension had been high between the two Lucilles since Buster had announced he'd been dating her. Although neither had addressed this, both had given indications of their true feelings. / Mom, you're ruining our fort.
jokes.setup: Narrator describes serious romantic tension between Lucille and Lucille 2 over Buster; hints at 'indications of their true feelings'
jokes.punchline: The 'indication' is Lucille apparently destroying Buster's fort — a deeply childish detail that recontextualizes Buster's relationship as almost nursery-level
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best gags. The narrator builds up 'indications of their true feelings' with real dramatic weight, then cuts to Buster complaining about his fort. It crystallizes Buster's entire character — an adult man-child trapped between two mother figures, literally playing with forts. Exceptional character integration. The deadpan narrator sell elevates it.

jokes.text: She changed him as a baby. / Okay. That's about the creepiest thing I've ever heard.
jokes.setup: Lucille explains why Buster dating Lucille 2 is inappropriate
jokes.punchline: The reason: Lucille 2 changed Buster's diapers — a revelation that is both objectively and subjectively creepy, delivered flat
jokes.explanation: Michael's 'That's about the creepiest thing I've ever heard' is given extra weight because Michael has heard a LOT of creepy things from this family. The flat delivery of the revelation followed by the equally flat confirmation works beautifully.

jokes.text: That's why she didn't look surprised.
jokes.setup: Michael processes the information that Lucille 2 changed Buster's diapers
jokes.punchline: He deduces, aloud, that this is why Lucille 2 wasn't surprised when Buster started dating her — implying Lucille 2 knew what she was getting into
jokes.explanation: Four words that do tremendous work. Michael connecting the dots in the most disturbing direction possible, and saying it out loud with complete calm, is a great straight-man moment. Has rewatch value because it implies a whole backstory.

jokes.text: You're the only child who chose a spouse I liked, and she's the one who had to die.
jokes.setup: Lucille laments Buster's inappropriate romance
jokes.punchline: She pivots to Michael's dead wife — with zero transition — as the one spouse she actually liked
jokes.explanation: Vintage Lucille — grief weaponized as a complaint. The line implies she cares more about having a daughter-in-law she approved of than about Michael's actual loss. 'She's the one who had to die' as though the wife died to spite Lucille is perfectly monstrous.

jokes.text: It's a good thing he's already got that little scooter.
jokes.setup: Michael tells Lucille that people want to break Gob's legs
jokes.punchline: Lucille's practical response: at least Gob already has a mobility scooter, so losing his legs won't be so bad
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. Lucille hears that her son's legs may be broken and responds with practical silver-lining thinking about his existing scooter. It's completely in character — her detachment from her children's physical safety is a running theme — and the specificity of 'that little scooter' is perfect. The line has Snip-snap-level economy and character integration. A true standout moment: absolutely no one else in television could deliver this line.

jokes.text: Where's my bed? / I put it in storage.
jokes.setup: Buster notices his bed is gone
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually reveals she put it in storage — as a power play to force Buster to choose between her and Lucille 2
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Lucille — removing her adult son's bed as a manipulation tactic. The casualness of 'I put it in storage' is the joke. She's not even slightly apologetic. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Okay. Before I leave this place for the last time ever...
jokes.setup: Buster dramatically declares he's leaving 'this place for the last time ever'
jokes.punchline: The show and audience know this is entirely hollow — Buster will absolutely not leave
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about Buster's codependency but fairly well-telegraphed. The 'last time ever' is funny because it's obviously false, but it doesn't have the surprise of the better Buster jokes in this episode.

jokes.text: I saw him last night at the Playtime Pizza Theater. On my date. Of which I have another one tonight. And I'm going to continue dating, Mom.
jokes.setup: Buster provides information about Gob's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: He pivots to an extended declaration that he IS dating and WILL continue to date — the defensiveness revealing how completely he's still answering to his mother even while asserting independence
jokes.explanation: 'Of which I have another one tonight' is a beautiful piece of defensive grammar. Buster is desperately proving his dating credentials to his mother while reporting a sighting of his brother. The unprompted escalation ('And I'm going to continue dating, Mom') is perfectly Buster.

jokes.text: Sounds a little bit like 'dating Mom.' / It's starting to feel a little like it.
jokes.setup: Buster says he's 'dating Mom' — meaning Lucille 2, who shares his mother's name
jokes.punchline: Michael hears 'dating Mom' as Buster dating their actual mother; Buster's 'It's starting to feel a little like it' confirms both readings are valid
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. The wordplay hinges on both Lucilles sharing a name, and Buster's confirmation that it 'feels a little like' dating his mother is both a punchline and a character revelation. The joke works on multiple levels simultaneously: the name pun, the psychological truth about Buster's attachment to his mother, and the dark implication about the Lucille 2 relationship. Very close to the episode's best.

jokes.text: Unfortunately, after a recent escape attempt, George Sr. had been put in solitary confinement. [30 seconds of silence/solitary scene]
jokes.setup: Lucille needs to see George Sr. to enlist his help with Buster's situation
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is in solitary — the entire trip is played out with no dialogue, presumably via glass and intercom, yielding no useful result
jokes.explanation: The scene's comedy is structural — the entire detour to the prison yields nothing because George Sr. is in solitary. The narrator's 'Unfortunately' framing signals it. More of a narrative joke than a punchline joke.

jokes.text: Even my own so-called girlfriend doesn't believe in me.
jokes.setup: Gob complains he's been blackballed by the Magicians' Alliance
jokes.punchline: 'So-called girlfriend' reveals Gob doesn't fully accept Marta as his girlfriend — despite her being his girlfriend — which given that he's cheating on her, carries extra irony
jokes.explanation: 'So-called girlfriend' is doing quiet work — Gob is simultaneously wronged by her doubts AND cheating on her. The character irony rewards attention.

jokes.text: A magician. / I think you're confusing scary with silly. This guy was frightening. He was in a limo.
jokes.setup: Michael describes Rollo as a 'mobster, a loan shark, something equally scary'
jokes.punchline: Gob says 'a magician' — and Michael's dismissal ('You're confusing scary with silly') doubles the joke when we realize Gob is right
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the terrifying Rollo is just a magician lands well. Michael's insistence that 'scary' and 'silly' are different — while Gob insists on 'magician' — is a great exchange. The limo detail Michael uses to prove gravitas is funny in retrospect.

jokes.text: He wants to take my legs. / I-I don't... / The legs... The bottom half of my saw-the-lady-in-half trick.
jokes.setup: 'Rollo wants your legs' sounds like a threat of violence; 'He wants to take my legs' confirms it — or does it?
jokes.punchline: The 'legs' are the bottom half of Gob's magic trick — the entire violent threat was about a prop
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's main misdirection setup. The word 'legs' has been doing double duty for seven minutes. The reveal is perfectly constructed — the tension built around physical harm collapses into a ridiculous magic prop dispute. The structure across the episode is excellent AD-style delayed payoff. Earns a high score for sustained misdirection executed cleanly.

jokes.text: No, not now. I'm showing him how to do the trick. / Two chicks curl up in a box. We call one the head and one the legs. / So that's how they do it. / God, I gotta stop giving these things away.
jokes.setup: Michael asks what happens in the sawing-in-half trick
jokes.punchline: Gob casually reveals the method ('two chicks in a box') and then catches himself — 'God, I gotta stop giving these things away' — the magician's code violated in real time
jokes.explanation: Gob is constitutionally incapable of keeping secrets — including his own magic secrets. The self-recrimination ('God, I gotta stop giving these things away') is perfect Gob: he knows his flaw but can't stop. Good character integration.

jokes.text: The old top half quit when she found out I was sleeping with the legs. / I guess word really gets around in there.
jokes.setup: Michael asks what happened to Gob's previous assistant (the top half)
jokes.punchline: She quit when she discovered Gob was sleeping with the woman playing 'the legs' — Michael's 'I guess word really gets around in there' treats the box as a gossip community
jokes.explanation: Michael's response — 'I guess word really gets around in there' — is the episode's cleverest line. He's playing along with the idea that two women curled up in a box constitute a community with its own gossip network. The deadpan extension of an absurd premise is exactly what great AD comedy looks like.

jokes.text: Would you give me a break, please? The legs are insanely jealous.
jokes.setup: Michael points out Gob is cheating on Marta with 'the legs'
jokes.punchline: Gob defends himself by noting 'the legs are insanely jealous' — treating the magic trick's bottom half as a romantic partner with emotions
jokes.explanation: Gob refers to his mistress only as 'the legs' throughout, which is both dehumanizing and absurd. 'The legs are insanely jealous' as an excuse is hilarious in its matter-of-factness. Very Gob — he presents this as a totally reasonable domestic complication.

jokes.text: I get absolutely nothing out of it. / Well, you get a false feeling of superiority. / I know. That is nice, but this time, it's not enough.
jokes.setup: Michael laments being a 'living saint' who gets nothing out of his goodness
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'you get a false feeling of superiority' is devastatingly accurate, and Michael's genuine 'I know. That is nice' confirms it while lamenting it's insufficient
jokes.explanation: An exceptional character comedy exchange. Gob — not usually the perceptive one — perfectly diagnoses Michael's psychology. Michael's response ('I know. That is nice') is simultaneously self-aware and self-damning: he KNOWS he does it for the superiority feeling, and he enjoys it, but this time it's not enough. This line could be the thesis of Michael's character. Highly quotable, strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: Why do I always have to be the one that has to set the good example for everyone? / Hey, guys. / Oh, that's why.
jokes.setup: Michael dramatically asks why he always has to be the moral example
jokes.punchline: George Michael (the actual good person) walks in; Michael's 'Oh, that's why' is his instant reminder — he has to be good because his son is watching
jokes.explanation: Structurally perfect. Michael's self-pity question ('Why do I always have to...') is answered by the universe immediately with a visual punchline (George Michael walks in), and Michael's two-word reply ('Oh, that's why') is a complete emotional arc compressed into the shortest possible form. This is textbook AD joke construction: setup, visual reveal, minimal dialogue, maximum meaning.

jokes.text: You have to be some sort of she-hulk to get this. / You just gotta really dig.
jokes.setup: Tobias and Lindsay are both wearing their new leather outfits
jokes.punchline: The leather is so tight Tobias says you need She-Hulk strength to get into it; Lindsay says 'you just gotta really dig' — implying she forced herself in
jokes.explanation: A functional visual gag about their leather outfits being comically tight/difficult. 'She-Hulk' is the funnier part; 'you just gotta really dig' is a decent physical comedy extension.

jokes.text: I got my hands on some money. I can't say how or when... or where my wedding ring is...
jokes.setup: Tobias triumphantly announces he has money
jokes.punchline: His immediate caveat — 'I can't say how or when... or where my wedding ring is' — implies he may have pawned or sold his wedding ring to get the money
jokes.explanation: The ellipsis in 'I can't say how or when... or where my wedding ring is' is expertly constructed — the missing wedding ring clearly connects to the mystery money, suggesting Tobias sold his ring without ever quite saying it. Dark, specific to the character, and economical. Very rewatch-friendly.

jokes.text: May I help you? / I hope so. I'm looking for something that says 'Dad likes leather.' / Something that says 'leather daddy'? / Oh, is there such a thing?
jokes.setup: Tobias goes to a leather shop looking for father-daughter bonding attire
jokes.punchline: 'Leather daddy' is gay subculture terminology; Tobias's delighted 'Oh, is there such a thing?' is obliviously enthusiastic
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias double entendre. He's searching for something wholesome ('Dad likes leather') and ends up in leather daddy territory. His complete obliviousness ('Oh, is there such a thing?') is the character in a nutshell — genuinely thrilled to find this exists, with zero self-awareness. Strong character integration; the joke only works because of Tobias's specific personality.

jokes.text: He said that, huh? Said that he's working, huh? Wow. Here? What else did he say? Did he tell you I gave him your message? Not that I'm the messenger, you know?
jokes.setup: Marta says Gob told her he was working at Michael's office today
jokes.punchline: Michael's increasingly panicked stalling — repeating words, piling on questions, pre-disavowing being 'the messenger' — as he tries not to reveal Gob's lies
jokes.explanation: Michael's verbal scrambling is a good cringe comedy moment. The repetition ('Said that, huh? Said that he's working, huh? Wow. Here?') mimics nervous stalling perfectly. The 'not that I'm the messenger' pre-emptive denial is very human and funny.

jokes.text: He said I was a treasure. / She's a treasure. And a once-in-a-lifetime woman.
jokes.setup: Marta happily reveals that Gob called her a treasure after Michael's speech to him
jokes.punchline: Michael hears his own words played back — he said these exact things to Gob about Marta, and Gob repeated them verbatim as his own — Michael murmurs them in recognition
jokes.explanation: Good callback joke. Gob has passed off Michael's heartfelt description of Marta as his own romantic poetry. Michael's murmured repetition ('She's a treasure. And a once-in-a-lifetime woman.') carries both irony and quiet devastation — he's watching his feelings be used as a prop.

jokes.text: Listen, Marta, Gob is... [long pause] ...a poet.
jokes.setup: Michael begins to tell Marta the truth about Gob — then stops
jokes.punchline: After a visibly agonizing pause, he completes the sentence with 'a poet' — the opposite of what he was about to say, and the opposite of what Gob is
jokes.explanation: The pause IS the joke. The silence represents Michael's entire moral collapse playing out in real time. 'A poet' as the conclusion is both maximally false (Gob is the least poetic person alive) and maximally funny as the word Michael reaches for when all else fails. The economy is exceptional — the structure depends on what ISN'T said.

jokes.text: I appreciate your time.
jokes.setup: Gob's watch — given to him by Michael — was actually delivered to Gob with a note from Michael saying 'I appreciate your time'
jokes.punchline: The watch joke: the message 'I appreciate your time' on a watch — a wordplay gift — is revealed to Marta. Gob also says it as a generic dismissal throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: The 'I appreciate your time' watch is a layered running gag — the phrase works as both a sincere note on a gift (appreciate = time/watch) and as Gob's casual dismissal of people. Discovering it was written on the watch adds the wordplay dimension. Classic AD double-meaning construction.

jokes.text: And so, for the first time, Michael set a plan in motion to take something that didn't belong to him.
jokes.setup: Michael decides to manipulate Marta and Gob's relationship for his own benefit
jokes.punchline: The narrator frames this moral lapse in the most portentous terms — 'for the first time' — while the viewer knows Michael is doing essentially what every Bluth does
jokes.explanation: The narrator's grave delivery of 'for the first time' is ironic commentary on Michael's self-image as different from his family. The framing of pursuing a woman as 'taking something that doesn't belong to him' mirrors the show's broader theft metaphor (chair, Gob's girlfriend, etc.). Good thematic density.

jokes.text: And you will get your legs back. / How? / You're asking me to reveal my trick?
jokes.setup: Michael negotiates with Rollo, promising the magician can have 'his legs back'
jokes.punchline: Rollo, a magician, instinctively responds 'You're asking me to reveal my trick?' — applying magician secrecy rules to a business negotiation
jokes.explanation: Rollo applying magician logic to a mundane situation is funny. The reflexive 'you're asking me to reveal my trick?' as a defense mechanism is good character comedy and ties back to Gob's own code earlier.

jokes.text: Has she got big cans?
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob he has a great girl for the assistant role — a favor to Michael
jokes.punchline: Gob's only question is whether she has 'big cans' — entirely missing that Michael is setting him up
jokes.explanation: Functional Gob joke but fairly crude and not particularly inventive. Works as character shorthand (Gob is shallow) but the laugh is cheap. The timing makes it functional but not memorable.

jokes.text: You're gonna put Marta onstage with the legs? / It's gonna blow up. Legs get upset. Marta gets upset. Marta leaves Gob, and I don't get shot.
jokes.setup: Lindsay hears Michael's plan and is skeptical
jokes.punchline: Michael's clinical breakdown of his scheme — 'legs get upset, Marta gets upset' — laid out with complete emotional detachment, ending with the practical note 'and I don't get shot'
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Michael's businesslike emotional calculus. 'And I don't get shot' as the final benefit — tacked on like a footnote — is the funniest element. The plan treats human feelings as dominoes to be toppled.

jokes.text: This stuff's kind of hot, isn't it? / I hadn't noticed.
jokes.setup: George Michael and Lindsay are both in their leather outfits at the leather store
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'This stuff's kind of hot' (temperature) might also be read as aesthetic; Lindsay's 'I hadn't noticed' is the adult, slightly awkward response
jokes.explanation: Minor beat. The ambiguity of 'hot' is low-key but the interaction between nephew and aunt in leather is inherently slightly uncomfortable in an AD-appropriate way.

jokes.text: After Michael claims he's become a new, selfish man, the leather jacket appears to squeak/cause pain. George Michael: 'Did that hurt?' Michael: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Michael is asserting his tougher, leather-wearing new identity.
jokes.punchline: The leather jacket clearly causes him discomfort, undermining his tough-guy posture. His denial ('No') while obviously in pain is classic Michael self-deception.
jokes.explanation: A solid physical gag. The leather jacket hurting him is appropriate — he's literally uncomfortable in his new identity. Economy is good but the joke is fairly predictable.

jokes.text: Look at us. Who'd wanna mess with any of us, huh?
jokes.setup: Tobias in leather gear tries to bond with the leather-clad family as a tough unit
jokes.punchline: The entire group is visually ridiculous — the opposite of threatening — and Tobias's pride in their collective toughness is completely deluded
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the leather gag. Tobias's delusion about the group's menacing appearance is a nice beat, and the question 'Who'd wanna mess with any of us?' is inherently funny because the answer is 'literally anyone.'

jokes.text: Did you get a job or something? / No. No, I didn't. Unless you consider 'World's Coolest Daddy' a job.
jokes.setup: Michael sees Tobias in leather and asks if he got a job
jokes.punchline: Tobias's non-job is 'World's Coolest Daddy' — a title he has apparently self-assigned, wearing leather gear he bought with possible wedding-ring money
jokes.explanation: 'World's Coolest Daddy' is a perfect Tobias non-answer. Michael's sincere question about employment met with Tobias's sincere pride about an imaginary honor is classic. The premise echoes joke #20 (Lindsay telling him he doesn't have a job) while adding new layers.

jokes.text: Yeah. I mean, we can't really change who we are anyway, right? / Wish you'd told me that before I threw away my Windbreaker.
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael he liked him better as he was — meaning George Michael doesn't need to be macho
jokes.punchline: George Michael reveals he threw away his Windbreaker to seem tougher — Michael's wisdom came one step too late
jokes.explanation: The Windbreaker is the perfect symbol — the least tough article of clothing imaginable. George Michael discarding it to seem cool, and having Michael's affirmation arrive one garbage can too late, is both touching and hilarious. 'Before I threw away my Windbreaker' is a quotable line precisely because of how specific and deflating 'Windbreaker' is.

jokes.text: Oh, you bought a chair. / Uh, no, actually, I borrowed it. Like we talked about. / Well, I thought we decided that was like stealing. / Is that where we landed on that?
jokes.setup: George Michael sees Michael has the leather chair — calling back to the episode's opening argument
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Is that where we landed on that?' — as if the moral conclusion of the opening conversation was ambiguous — is perfect denial
jokes.explanation: 'Is that where we landed on that?' is a beautifully evasive line. Michael genuinely seems to be questioning whether the moral conclusion of a conversation he started is binding. It's a politician's dodge from a man who claims to be different from his family. Strong callback structure — the episode's A-plot is bookended by this chair.

jokes.text: As Michael was becoming more selfish, his father was choosing a more pious path.
jokes.setup: Michael is taking a work chair and manipulating his brother's relationship
jokes.punchline: The narrator announces George Sr. — a man in prison for massive fraud — is 'choosing a more pious path,' cutting to what is clearly a prison religious conversion bit
jokes.explanation: The ironic parallel — Michael the 'good guy' becoming more selfish while George Sr. the criminal becomes pious — is a nice structural joke. The narrator framing it as parallel moral journeys with inverted directions is good AD construction.

jokes.text: Why is there a piece of shoe on your head?
jokes.setup: George Sr. is in prison wearing some kind of religious head covering (yarmulke or similar)
jokes.punchline: Lucille describes it as 'a piece of shoe' — her complete ignorance of or contempt for religious items reduces a potentially moving moment to absurdity
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'A piece of shoe' is specific enough to be hilarious — she's identified the material (leather) but completely missed the context. It perfectly characterizes Lucille's contempt and ignorance simultaneously. The economy is superb: five words, zero wasted.

jokes.text: This is a... Well, it's a reminder that the divine presence is always above me.
jokes.setup: Lucille demands an explanation for George Sr.'s religious headgear
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s sincere spiritual explanation — that God is above him — is funny because (a) he's a convicted fraudster, (b) the prison setting, and (c) his spiritual awakening is clearly new and untested
jokes.explanation: Works as setup for the scene but the irony (criminal converting to religion) is fairly standard comedy territory. The line itself isn't particularly crafted; the humor comes from context.

jokes.text: Well, more importantly, Buster's been humping the widow Austero.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is in the middle of explaining his spiritual journey
jokes.punchline: Lucille completely ignores the spiritual conversation to drop 'Well, more importantly, Buster's been humping the widow Austero' — 'humping' as her vocabulary choice is both crude and period-inappropriate coming from her
jokes.explanation: The word 'humping' from Lucille is the joke — the show mines the disconnect between her patrician presentation and her blunt language. The pivot from George Sr.'s spiritual moment to this completely overrides the scene's mood. 'Well, more importantly' as the transition is also funny: clearly not more important in any cosmic sense, but to Lucille, absolutely is.

jokes.text: He stays there sometimes until 7:00, 8:00 at night. Peanut brittle on his breath. Is she the one who's going to take him to the dentist? / She already has.
jokes.setup: Lucille describes Buster's visits to Lucille 2 with escalating details
jokes.punchline: The peanut brittle → dentist chain is both absurd parenting-level concern for an adult man AND confirmed: Lucille 2 HAS taken him to the dentist, meaning she's fully assumed Lucille's role
jokes.explanation: Excellent. Lucille's concern about Buster's dental care would be appropriate for a six-year-old, not a grown man. The chain (peanut brittle → dentist concern → 'She already has') escalates beautifully. 'She already has' confirms that Lucille 2 is literally performing Lucille's maternal duties, which is both funny and darkly on-point about Buster's psychology.

jokes.text: 'May you live. May you be well. I give you my blessing.' / Wow! Really? / Are you out of your mind?
jokes.setup: George Sr., in his new pious mode, gives Buster a blessing for his relationship
jokes.punchline: Buster is delighted ('Wow! Really?'); Lucille is horrified ('Are you out of your mind?') — the blessing only encourages the behavior Lucille is trying to stop
jokes.explanation: The reversal — George Sr.'s new piety leading him to bless the exact relationship Lucille wants stopped — is a good structural joke. 'Are you out of your mind?' from Lucille is perfect. George Sr.'s spiritual journey immediately working against his wife is ironic.

jokes.text: Can you get me some of that peanut brittle?
jokes.setup: After giving a holy blessing, George Sr. has one practical follow-up
jokes.punchline: He asks for peanut brittle — the snack that just came up in the context of Buster's childlike relationship — undermining any spiritual gravitas instantly
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the scene. The callback to peanut brittle — mentioned in the context of Buster being a child — used by George Sr. as a prison snack request deflates his spiritual pose completely. It's also a callback that connects the prison storyline to the Buster storyline in a funny, unexpected way.

jokes.text: Where's the top half? / I have to teach her this trick. She's gotta fold her knees. / It's a fantastic trick. I don't want you to worry though. I've already told her.
jokes.setup: Gob panics backstage about his trick logistics; Michael covers by telling Gob he's told the assistant
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I don't want you to worry though. I've already told her' — 'already told her' being his setup for exposing Gob's affair, which Gob doesn't realize
jokes.explanation: Works as dramatic irony — Michael's calm reassurance carries a second meaning. The rewatch value is good because on second viewing Michael's 'I've already told her' is clearly menacing.

jokes.text: Where to, mate? / The Gothic Castle. / Gothic Asshole? / That's what I said.
jokes.setup: Tobias directs his cab to the Gothic Castle (the magic venue)
jokes.punchline: The cabdriver mishears 'Gothic Castle' as 'Gothic Asshole'; Tobias confirms 'That's what I said' — either he didn't notice the difference or he genuinely said 'Asshole'
jokes.explanation: A deceptively well-constructed joke. The ambiguity is the genius: did Tobias actually say 'Gothic Asshole'? His confirmation ('That's what I said') suggests yes. The joke implies Tobias, in his leather daddy gear, may have instinctively navigated to a different kind of venue. Strong character integration — only works because of Tobias's specific, oblivious relationship with gay culture.

jokes.text: And ended up in the wrong location. / Oh, I am glad I didn't go with that outfit. / Yes, hello. I am looking for the magic. / Danke schön.
jokes.setup: Tobias ends up at an apparently different location than the Gothic Castle
jokes.punchline: His reaction ('glad I didn't go with that outfit'), his question ('I am looking for the magic'), and someone saying 'Danke schön' all suggest he ended up at a gay leather bar — and is taking it in stride
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the Gothic Castle/Asshole joke. Tobias at a German leather bar asking 'I am looking for the magic' (while in leather daddy gear, surrounded by people in leather) is a layered Tobias joke — he's technically accurate ('looking for magic') while completely in the wrong place, completely unfazed. 'Danke schön' as the response implies he's been welcomed. Strong visual gag from a subtitle-only transcript.

jokes.text: Hello, Lucille. / Hello, Lucille. Having a good time with my son?
jokes.setup: Both Lucilles encounter each other at the Gothic Castle; each greets the other by name
jokes.punchline: The comic absurdity of two women with the same name in direct confrontation over a shared man-child, each saying the other's name with a different inflection
jokes.explanation: The two-Lucilles setup is the show's ongoing gag and this is a functional usage. 'Having a good time with my son?' from Lucille loads 'my son' with possessiveness that drives the drama-comedy.

jokes.text: You know, he's damaged goods. He was born with a hole in his heart. / Now listen to me, Lucille. I'm gonna fill that hole, 'cause we're in love.
jokes.setup: Lucille tries to discourage Lucille 2 by listing Buster's defects, starting with a literal birth defect
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2's 'I'm gonna fill that hole' turns a medical detail into a declaration of love — unintentionally but perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: A good dark comedy exchange. Lucille uses 'hole in his heart' as a defect; Lucille 2 turns it into a love metaphor. The literalism of 'fill that hole' against the cardiac origin is nicely weird.

jokes.text: You're no more in love with him than I am.
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 insists she's in love with Buster
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'You're no more in love with him than I am' — meaning 'not at all' — but it also sounds like she's confirming her own (inappropriate) love for her son
jokes.explanation: A genuinely brilliant double reading. Lucille intends it as 'you're not in love,' but the line equally reads as 'I love him as much as you do' — which given their dynamic, is disturbing and funny. The ambiguity is intentional and pitch-perfect. Economy is excellent — a single sentence contains a complete character study.

jokes.text: Okay. We're all saying some things we're going to regret.
jokes.setup: Lucille and Lucille 2 are fighting over Buster
jokes.punchline: Buster intervenes with the most measured, diplomatic possible response — as though this is a normal argument that will produce mutual regret, not a profoundly weird situation
jokes.explanation: Buster's peacemaking instinct is funny here because the situation is so far beyond 'things we'll regret' that his intervention is absurdly insufficient. His applying normal relationship wisdom to an abnormal situation is good character comedy.

jokes.text: Listen, I got you something... for being such a good guy... not that it's a surprise, you always are. / Oh, well, uh... / My watch. / I want you to have it. / Well, you've stolen so many things from me over the years. This is the first time you've ever given something back.
jokes.setup: Gob gives Michael his watch as a thank-you for being a good brother
jokes.punchline: Michael's response acknowledges Gob's lifetime of taking from him — 'you've stolen so many things from me over the years, this is the first time you've ever given something back' — at the moment Michael is literally planning to steal Gob's girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Rich with irony — Michael is touched by Gob giving something back while plotting to take something from Gob. 'You've stolen so many things from me over the years' has added resonance because Michael is about to steal the most significant thing (a relationship) from Gob. The watch gift creates maximum dramatic irony.

jokes.text: Where's the leather jacket? / It's like you said... you can't change who you are. So what? So I don't have any hair on my arms or legs. So what?
jokes.setup: George Michael told Michael 'you can't change who you are'; George Michael took it seriously and abandoned his attempt to be macho
jokes.punchline: George Michael's application of the wisdom leads him to accept he has no arm or leg hair — citing this as the thing about himself he's at peace with
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff. 'You can't change who you are' was Michael's self-justifying wisdom (for being good); George Michael applies it literally to his lack of body hair, which perfectly circles back to the Maeby embarrassment that started the subplot. 'So what? So I don't have any hair on my arms or legs. So what?' is repetition comedy at its best — the second 'So what?' does more work than the first.

jokes.text: You'd better tell Gob, the legs aren't coming. / Oh, my God. / I can't believe the legs would screw him like this. This is his one last chance. They'll never let him in the Alliance again. / You have to do something. This is so important to me and Gob.
jokes.setup: The 'legs' (Gob's mistress) have bailed on the show
jokes.punchline: Marta tells Michael that 'the legs aren't coming' — not knowing 'the legs' is a woman Gob is sleeping with — and urges him to help. Michael's plan collapses: Marta is now his unknowing accomplice in resolving the crisis
jokes.explanation: Good structural payoff. Marta — the intended victim of Michael's scheme — becomes the person who asks Michael to save Gob's show. The double meaning of 'the legs aren't coming' (prop and person) collapses Michael's plan in an ironic way. He has to rescue Gob instead of exposing him.

jokes.text: Where am I gonna get a pair of beautiful women's legs on such short notice?
jokes.setup: Marta urgently needs someone to fill in as 'the legs' for Gob's trick
jokes.punchline: Michael asking 'where am I gonna get a pair of beautiful women's legs on such short notice?' — the answer being obviously himself (implied by what follows)
jokes.explanation: The setup for one of the episode's best visual gags. 'Beautiful women's legs' as a description of what Michael needs is both urgent and absurd, and the implication that he will supply them himself is the setup for the magic trick reveal.

jokes.text: So, the trick went forward as planned. / [Gob performs the trick; Michael is curled up in the box as 'the legs']
jokes.setup: 'Where am I gonna get a pair of beautiful women's legs on such short notice?'
jokes.punchline: Michael IS the legs — the visual of Michael Bluth, company president and self-styled noble man, curled up in a magic box as Gob's assistant
jokes.explanation: The episode's physical comedy climax. Michael — who started the episode arguing about the ethics of a chair — ends up literally contorted in a magic prop as his brother's legs. The narrator's 'as planned' is perfect irony (nothing went as planned). It's the visual embodiment of all his compromises. Exceptional structure and character integration — this could only be Michael.

jokes.text: Wiggle those sexy gams, lady.
jokes.setup: Gob commands his legs assistant to wiggle seductively
jokes.punchline: He's commanding Michael — his brother — to wiggle his legs as 'sexy gams'; Michael is presumably complying
jokes.explanation: 'Wiggle those sexy gams, lady' addressed to Michael (hidden as the legs) is a great line. 'Gams' is archaic slang, 'lady' is wrong on multiple levels, and the instruction to be sexy is directed at Michael's actual legs — which we know from the episode are approximately as hairy as his teenage son's. Strong payoff.

jokes.text: I'm okay with myself. I'm okay with myself.
jokes.setup: Michael is curled up in a magic prop being instructed to wiggle his legs as 'sexy gams'
jokes.punchline: His mantra of 'I'm okay with myself' — repeated — suggests he's very much not okay, and is trying to survive the most dignity-stripping moment of the episode
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The repetition is key — saying it once is denial; saying it twice is a breakdown. The line encapsulates Michael's entire arc: he claims moral superiority ('I'm okay with myself') while being literally folded into a box playing sexy magician legs. Economy is nearly perfect — five words twice, maximum character revelation.

jokes.text: Mom, did you see it? I'm back in. I've got the career back.
jokes.setup: Gob triumphantly announces his career comeback after the show
jokes.punchline: His definition of 'career' is being readmitted to the Magicians' Alliance — one step above being blackballed from entertaining children — and he's treating this with the gravity of a major comeback
jokes.explanation: Functional Gob character moment but not particularly crafted. The comedy is entirely in the stakes mismatch (the Magicians' Alliance is a tiny world), which has been established throughout.

jokes.text: And when Lucille noticed how happy Marta had made Gob, she realized how important love was to her children.
jokes.setup: The scene turns emotional as Gob and Marta reunite
jokes.punchline: The narrator's straight-faced suggestion that Lucille 'realized how important love was to her children' — Lucille, who has shown zero capacity for this realization throughout the episode — is pure irony
jokes.explanation: The narrator setting up Lucille's epiphany with total sincerity, knowing the audience knows it's false, is a standard AD narrator move. Works but is among the more formulaic uses of the technique in this episode.

jokes.text: I was wondering if you'd like to play bridge with me next week?
jokes.setup: Lucille approaches Lucille 2 — after a confrontation — appearing ready to make amends about Buster
jokes.punchline: Her olive branch is simply asking about bridge — she's not addressing Buster at all, just resuming their friendship on her terms without acknowledging the conflict
jokes.explanation: The joke is in what's NOT said. Lucille pivots from their fight about Buster to asking about bridge — a social maneuver that accepts the situation without acknowledging it. It's a completely Lucille resolution: pragmatic, conflict-avoidant in form while controlling in substance.

jokes.text: Could you just... just separate?
jokes.setup: Both Lucilles are embracing warmly while Buster stands between them
jokes.punchline: Buster's weak plea for the two women to separate — said while he's presumably sandwiched between/around them — is both physically comic and psychologically revealing (he's more uncomfortable by their closeness than empowered by it)
jokes.explanation: 'Could you just... just separate?' with the hesitation is a great button line for the Buster subplot. He's supposed to be the prize in this conflict but ends up asking the two women to please stop hugging around him. The pause before 'just' suggests genuine discomfort.

jokes.text: Michael watched as the brother he swore to stop helping enjoyed the girlfriend he helped him reunite with.
jokes.setup: Michael's scheme to sabotage Gob's relationship backfired and he ended up helping Gob instead
jokes.punchline: The narrator summarizes the irony with maximum dryness — Michael's bad-guy plan resulted in the same outcome as his good-guy mode
jokes.explanation: The narrator's thematic summary is well-constructed — Michael can't escape being the good guy even when trying to be selfish. Works as an ironic button on the episode's arc. Good economy.

jokes.text: This really worked better with the chair.
jokes.setup: Michael is back at work, presumably trying to recline or relax — the stolen chair has presumably been returned or is inadequate
jokes.punchline: Michael's quiet admission that whatever he's sitting on now doesn't work as well — a callback to the entire chair saga that bookended the episode
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode button. The chair has been the through-line metaphor for Michael's moral compromise all episode. His quiet acknowledgment — 'This really worked better with the chair' — suggests he returned the chair (moral victory) but now misses it (defeat). The line is ambiguous enough to be read as him missing the chair or missing some other part of his brief foray into selfishness. Highly economical, deeply character-specific, excellent callback structure.

jokes.text: That was just my girlfriend and my nephew. / Well, you're out.
jokes.setup: 'On The Next Arrested Development' — Rollo asks how Gob got two Alliance-approved assistants; Gob reveals the truth
jokes.punchline: Gob used his girlfriend (Marta) and nephew (George Michael) — violating Alliance rules — and is immediately expelled again
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed 'On the next' gag. The callback reveals that Michael's entire scheme — Marta as the legs — exposed the fact that she and George Michael were non-Alliance, which gets Gob expelled again immediately. All of Michael's moral compromise achieved exactly nothing. The economy ('Well, you're out') is superb.

jokes.text: And Tobias gets some good news. / I got a gig.
jokes.setup: 'On the next' tease: Tobias announces 'I got a gig' as good news after ending up at the wrong location
jokes.punchline: The implication — given he ended up at a German leather bar asking 'where is the magic' — is that his 'gig' may not be what he intended
jokes.explanation: A strong 'On the Next' button for the Tobias subplot. 'I got a gig' as good news, given where he ended up, implies the gig is at the leather bar — a perfectly Tobias outcome where he stumbles into something wildly inappropriate and treats it as a career win.

tmdb_overview: Michael cannot go through with his plan to break up Gob and Marta. A thug magician wants to take Gob's "legs". Maeby wears all leather to get back at her mother, and Lucille decides to confront Buster's relationship with Lucille 2.
insight: Castle-storming plot carries 48 jokes across 22 minutes despite middling impact scores.
